title: Curvature-Controlled Infrared Regularization of Crystalline Membranes
abstract: We show that negative Gaussian curvature regularizes the infrared sector of crystalline membranes. In a covariant formulation of embedded elasticity, the Green strain contains a symmetry-required linear coupling between the normal phonon and extrinsic curvature. Integrating out the in-plane phonons converts this coupling into a finite quadratic contribution to the inverse flexural response. Anomalous roughening is thereby replaced by curvature-controlled saturation, and the mechanism survives on minimal hyperbolic patches. Hyperbolic geometry preempts anomalous elasticity before the flat infrared regime is reached, implying the absence of a crumpling phase. The same Gaussian-order coupling admits sound propagation in the infrared.
summary: This theoretical work shows that the intrinsic negative Gaussian curvature of crystalline membranes prevents them from undergoing anomalous elastic collapse (crumpling). By analyzing the effective energy derived from integrating out in-plane phonon modes, the authors demonstrate that the membrane roughness saturates to a finite value determined by the local curvature, resolving a long-standing theoretical paradox.
main_problem: The paper addresses the anomalous infrared behavior of crystalline membranes, where standard elasticity theory predicts divergences and a crumpling phase when assuming flat reference geometries.
main_result: Negative Gaussian curvature regularizes the infrared sector, leading to a finite, curvature-controlled saturation of membrane roughness and precluding the anomalous crumpling phase.
method: The analysis uses a covariant formulation of embedded elasticity, integrating out in-plane phonon modes to derive an effective quadratic Hamiltonian for the normal fluctuations.
model_or_system: The system is crystalline membranes modeled by continuum elasticity theory, incorporating both stretching and bending energies (Helfrich-Canham type). The analysis is performed using Riemann normal coordinates.
key_observables: Static structure factor S(q) and the mean-squared normal displacement W2(L), which are shown to saturate to finite values.
important_parameters: Negative Gaussian curvature (K < 0), the crossover scale (l_c), and the local elastic moduli.
main_assumptions: The regularization derived is a strict lower bound based on truncating the local geometric expansion, and the analysis relies on specific boundary conditions for integration by parts.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the saturation of the mean-squared normal displacement W2(L) for hyperbolic curvature radii, showing a transition from anomalous flat growth to a finite plateau at the curvature scale l_c.
paper_structure: The paper develops the elastic energy functional, analyzes the contribution of tangential vs. normal deformations to the phonon integral, and finally derives the effective Hamiltonian by integrating out in-plane phonons to determine the long-wavelength response.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter geometry, the mathematical techniques involving effective Hamiltonians derived from integrating out high-frequency modes (phonons) and the resulting regularization mechanisms are highly relevant to understanding effective theories in quantum field theory and many-body physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The mean-square normal displacement W 2 ∞(L) is shown for hyperbolic curvature radii RK = 50, 100, 200 nm with graphene-inspired elastic parameters. Flat anomalous growth at small L (blue region) is cut off at the curvature scale ℓc, after which W 2 ∞(L) saturates to a finite value W 2 ∞ (red region). The dotted and dashed lines mark the satura- tion plateaus and ℓc values, respectively. The inset shows the normalized saturation deficit, the dashed line indicates the universal large-scale approach (L/ℓc)−2.
